page: Amazon Web Services m5a.12xlarge is a general-purpose instance powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor. It features 48 vCPUs and 24 physical cores based on the x86_64 AMD EPYC 7571 processor running at 2.5 GHz. The system is equipped with 192.0 GB of DDR4 memory operating at 3 GHz, yielding a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPUs, relying instead on network-attached storage and a baseline network bandwidth of 10 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates a clear performance tradeoff between single-core and multi-core execution. Single-core performance is consistently low, with stress-ng single-core, Geekbench single-core, and single-threaded compression tasks scoring in the bottom 10%. Conversely, multi-core workloads, such as stress-ng multi-core, Geekbench multi-core, and multi-threaded bzip3 decompression, perform in the average tier. Memory bandwidth tests for both small and large blocks also align with average tier expectations. Notably, the CPU encryption test under Passmark registers strong performance in the top 25%, aided by hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI instruction sets.

Given its hardware profile, the m5a.12xlarge offers qualitative cost efficiency for workloads that benefit from high core density and balanced memory allocation rather than raw single-thread speed. It is suited for multi-threaded applications, static web serving, database operations, and software compilation. However, it is less suitable for single-threaded tasks or medium-to-large language model inference, where text generation speeds fall into the weak or untested categories.
description: Amazon Web Services m5a.12xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 48 vCPUs and 24 physical cores powered by an AMD EPYC 7571 processor running at 2.5 GHz. Built on the Nitro hypervisor, it includes 192.0 GB of DDR4 memory at 3 GHz, providing a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. The instance lacks local storage and GPUs, but offers a 10 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Performance benchmarks reveal a distinct tradeoff: single-core metrics, including Geekbench and stress-ng single-core, fall into the bottom 10%, whereas multi-core operations, database tasks, and memory bandwidth consistently rank in the average tier. Notably, cryptographic tasks perform strongly in the top 25% due to hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI support. This instance is suited for multi-threaded workloads, static web serving, and database operations where high resource density is required without needing peak single-thread performance.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m5a.12xlarge is a general-purpose instance with 48 vCPUs, 192 GB DDR4 RAM, and an AMD EPYC processor, offering balanced multi-core performance and strong cryptographic capabilities.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m5a.12xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 48 vCPUs, 192 GB RAM, and an AMD EPYC CPU on the Nitro hypervisor.
tagline: A general-purpose virtual server featuring forty-eight vCPUs, balanced memory allocation, and hardware-accelerated cryptography for multi-threaded workloads.
bullet_points: Powered by a 24-core AMD EPYC 7571 processor running at 2.5 GHz
Equipped with 192.0 GB of DDR4 memory and 10 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
Strong cryptographic performance supported by hardware-accelerated AES and SHA_NI
Average multi-core and memory bandwidth benchmark results
Tradeoff of lower single-core performance compared to multi-core capabilities
Best suited for multi-threaded applications, database operations, and static web serving
